result:
I take the night in small sips. The city breathes out light like a sleeping animal, and I walk its ribs—avenues, alleys, the soft hiss of tires on wet asphalt. I can smell wet brick and something sweet, like jasmine that learned to bloom at midnight. A bus sighs at the curb, doors open, nobody gets on, and it rolls away. A cat considers me, decides I am harmless, slinks into shadow. I step around a puddle that holds two moons, the real one and its streetlight twin.

When the clock on the bakery reads 11:46, I think about how this hour still belongs to us. Not the day, with its agendas and screens, not the early morning rush, but this quiet pocket where time feels borrowed. The jacaranda petals are a smear of lavender under my shoes, and somewhere a radio leaks a song about leaving town, its chorus half a block ahead of me. I turn a corner and find a courtyard full of bicycles and a single, stubborn fountain that refuses to keep quiet.

By the time I reach my door, the mug of tea on the table has a skin on it, a little cloud of steam that cools into memory. I sit and breathe, counting the spaces between sounds, the pauses where my heart decides it is okay. The night doesn’t speak, but it listens, and when I finally stand, it lets me go.

A cat considers me, decides I am harmless, slinks into shadow. I step around a puddle that holds two moons, the real one and its streetlight twin.

When the clock on the bakery reads 11:46, I think about how this hour still belongs to us. Not the day, with its agendas and screens, not the early morning rush, but this quiet pocket where time feels borrowed. The jacaranda petals are a smear of lavender under my shoes, and somewhere a radio leaks a song about leaving town, its chorus half a block ahead of me. I turn a corner and find a courtyard full of bicycles and a single, stubborn fountain that refuses to keep quiet.

By the time I reach my door, the mug of tea on the table has a skin on it, a little cloud of steam that cools into memory. I sit and breathe, counting the spaces between sounds, the pauses where my heart decides it is okay. The night doesn’t speak, but it listens, and when I finally stand, it lets me go.
